nativeRange.habitat: Small blackwater streams and swamps in the Madre de Dios River basin; slow-moving or still water over leaf litter and sandy substrate with dense riparian vegetation and overhanging roots

decorPreferences: numerous clay or coconut-shell caves for spawning
leaf litter (Indian almond leaves, oak leaves) to generate tannins and blackwater conditions
driftwood and bogwood for cover and pH buffering
dense planting (Java fern, Amazon sword, Vallisneria) to break lines of sight between females
peat filtration or blackwater extract to achieve target pH and soft water

diet.notes: Primarily insectivorous/carnivorous in the wild. In captivity, thrives on varied live and frozen foods: Artemia (brine shrimp), Cyclops, Daphnia, bloodworms, micro worms, and small live or frozen invertebrates. Will reluctantly accept high-quality micro-pellets and fine granules, but live and frozen foods are strongly preferred and necessary for conditioning breeders. Feed multiple small portions daily; uneaten food degrades water quality rapidly in the soft, low-pH blackwater setups this species requires.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (cardinal tetra, ember tetra, rummy-nose tetra) sharing blackwater parameters
small Corydoras (sterbai, habrosus) adapted to warm soft water
dwarf shrimp (Neocaridina) may be tolerated in well-planted tanks but fry risk exists
avoidWith: other Apistogramma species or males of the same species in small tanks
boisterous or fast-moving mid-water fish that compete for food
hard-water cichlids incompatible with acidic parameters

imageCandidates.notes: Species type specimen image on FishBase summary page; verify license before use.

summary: A rare Peruvian blackwater dwarf cichlid from the Madre de Dios basin, named after co-expedition leader Jorge Andrés Urteaga Cavero. Males reach ~4 cm and display subtle metallic teal coloration; females turn vivid yellow during brood care. A haremic cave-spawner requiring soft, acidic water and expert-level water management.
careNotes: Apistogramma urteagai is a blackwater obligate from the Madre de Dios River basin in southeastern Peru. It is among the smallest Apistogramma species (males to ~4.1 cm SL) and is considered rare in the aquarium trade, maintained primarily by specialist dwarf-cichlid keepers working with wild-collected or F1 strains. Strict water chemistry management is non-negotiable: pH 4.5–6.5, GH 0–8, KH 0–4. Peat filtration, Indian almond leaves, alder cones, or commercial blackwater extract should be used to achieve and maintain the required soft, acidic chemistry. Even brief exposure to hard or alkaline water stresses the fish and suppresses breeding. A tank of at least 60 L with a 60 cm footprint is the practical minimum for a 1 male + 2–3 female harem group; more space (80–100 L) is strongly preferred to allow each female to establish her own territory around a spawning cave. The substrate should be fine sand to support natural sifting behavior. Provide an abundance of caves (clay pots, coconut shells, ceramic tubes) and dense planting or driftwood to break sightlines between females, who will defend their clutches aggressively from conspecifics and other intruders. Tankmates, if included, must be small, non-competitive species that thrive in blackwater conditions — cardinal tetras, pencilfish, and Otocinclus are the most commonly cited companions. Males display a subdued metallic teal mid-body when courting; females transform to brilliant yellow with bold black markings when guarding eggs or fry. The species is grouped in the A. regani group, resticulosa-complex. Conditioning for breeding requires consistent live and frozen foods and pristine water quality.
breedingNotes: A. urteagai is a haremic cave-spawner: a single dominant male courts and spawns with multiple females simultaneously, each claiming her own territory around a spawning site. Females deposit eggs on the ceiling of a cave (inverted clay pot or coconut-shell cave) and provide exclusive brood care; the male plays no direct role in fry protection. Clutches are modest in size for an Apistogramma. Eggs typically hatch within 48–72 hours at 26–28°C, and fry are free-swimming within 5–7 days. The female guards and fans the fry actively for approximately four weeks after free-swimming. First foods for fry: freshly hatched brine shrimp nauplii and micro worms. Breeders report the species as unusually prolific and attentive as parents compared to other Apistogramma. Soft, acidic water (pH 5.0–6.0, near-zero hardness) is reported to improve hatch rates and sex ratios. Remove or partition the male if female–female aggression escalates. The PVAS breeding account (2024) documented 150–200 juveniles from a single breeding group, underscoring this species’ reproductive productivity when water conditions are met.

primary.name: FishBase — Apistogramma urteagai
primary.notes: Primary taxonomic and biometric reference. Confirmed adult size (max 4.1 cm SL males), order Cichliformes, family Cichlidae, native range (Madre de Dios River basin, Peru, Amazon drainage), water parameters (pH 5.0–6.0, dH 6–10, temperature 22–29°C), habitat (small streams and swamps), and reproductive note (eggs deposited on cave ceiling, tended by female).

additional.name: PVAS — Apistogramma urteagai: Collecting, Keeping and Breeding a Wild Apisto
additional.notes: Detailed first-person hobbyist account of wild-collected specimens. Confirmed haremic polygamous behavior (1 male, multiple females), ease of breeding when water conditions met, prolific nature (150–200 juveniles across breeding group), use of clay pots and driftwood, fry raised on brine shrimp nauplii, and blackwater/acidic water requirements. Primary behavioral and breeding reference.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Care Guide for Apistogramma Dwarf Cichlids
additional.notes: General Apistogramma husbandry guidance used to cross-check tank size (20 gal / ~75 L minimum for a pair or small group), cave-spawning behavior, blackwater setup with Indian almond leaves and tannins, fine sand substrate, and compatible tankmates. No dedicated A. urteagai page; general guidance applied to species.

additional.name: Aquarium Glaser — Apistogramma urteagai
additional.notes: Wholesale/trade note confirming Madre de Dios, Peru origin, undemanding relative to many Apistogramma, and polygamous harem behavior. Noted as offered only occasionally in the trade. Cross-referenced coloration: male metallic teal mid-body, females vivid yellow during brood care.

additional.name: AquaInfo — Apistogramma urteagai
additional.notes: Independent hobbyist reference confirming blackwater/acidic requirement (peat filtration recommended), polygamous harem ratio (3 females to 1 male), cave spawning, female-only brood care for ~4 weeks post free-swimming, and species rarity in trade. Water parameters (pH 5.0–6.5, GH 0–8, KH 0–4) cross-checked here.

fish.breedingNotes: Cave-spawner; female deposits eggs on cave ceiling and provides sole brood care for ~4 weeks. Requires soft acidic blackwater (pH 5.0–6.0, GH near zero) for reliable spawning and healthy sex ratios. Haremic setup (1 male, 2–3 females) with individual cave territories per female. Prolific once conditions are correct; fry accept brine shrimp nauplii.
